summary: AI-native software factory for enterprise SDLC control.
editor_note: The bet is that AI coding needs an operating layer before it needs another agent. Software Factory exposes 5 modules: requirements, blueprints, work orders, tests, and feedback, plus docs, pricing, and an audit trail. Unlike prompt-first coding assistants, 8090 moves intent and oversight upstream so business leaders can see what agents are building.
why_featured: $135M Series A for AI SDLC control

product_evidence.claim: Software Factory is presented as an AI-native software development control plane that coordinates teams and AI agents with visibility and auditability from intent to code.
The product page exposes five modules: Requirements, Blueprints, Work Orders, Tests, and Feedback.
The official Series A post says 8090 works with regulated industries including healthcare, insurance, life sciences, aerospace, energy, manufacturing, financial services, and the U.S. government.

market_context.primary_user: Enterprise engineering, product, and transformation teams adopting AI-assisted software development.
market_context.differentiation: 8090 emphasizes requirements, documentation, work orders, tests, and auditability rather than only code completion.
market_context.why_now: The $135M Series A and regulated-enterprise positioning signal investor demand for governed AI software creation beyond individual developer copilots.

risks.claim: The open question is whether enterprise buyers will let an external software factory sit close enough to requirements, architecture, and code to become the system of record.
risks.basis: VentureDex editorial assessment based on the official product page, docs, and Series A announcement.
